stories.thread: History and Rationale of the Ban
stories.story: Since Iran's Islamic Revolution in 1979, women have been barred from attending most sports events involving men. Iranian authorities imposed the ban, deeming that mixed crowds watching games together was un-Islamic. During the ensuing decades, the crowds at soccer games, Iran's most popular sport, have been all male. Iranian women were briefly permitted to attend volleyball matches under the moderate President Mohammad Khatami, but the ban was reinstated in 2005 after the more hard-line Mahmoud Ahmadinejad came to power.
key_facts: Ban on women attending men's sports events since 1979
Imposed after Islamic Revolution, deemed un-Islamic
Crowds at soccer games have been all male
Briefly permitted for volleyball under Mohammad Khatami
Ban reinstated in 2005 under Mahmoud Ahmadinejad

stories.story: The situation regarding women attending men's sports events in Iran appears set to improve in the coming months. Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi announced that a plan to allow "women and families" to enter sports stadiums will come into effect in the next year. He stated that the ban will be lifted for some events, specifically indoor sports events. However, it isn't clear exactly which games women will be able to attend. The rules won't change for all matches because some sports are mainly related to men and "families are not interested in attending" them.
key_facts: Situation set to improve in coming months
Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi announced plan
"Women and families" to enter stadiums in the next year
Ban to be lifted for some events, specifically indoor sports
Rules won't change for all matches due to lack of family interest in some men's sports
explicit_unknowns: Exactly which games women will be able to attend

stories.story: The Iranian government has come under pressure from international sports officials over the restrictions on women attending men's sports events. Last month, FIFA President Sepp Blatter called on Iran to end its "intolerable" ban on women attending soccer matches, saying the situation "cannot continue." The ban on women attending matches was widely seen as a major impediment to Iran's chances of securing the 2019 edition of soccer's Asian Cup. Iran had been in the running to host the tournament, but it was awarded to the United Arab Emirates.
key_facts: Iranian government under pressure from international sports officials
FIFA President Sepp Blatter called for end to "intolerable" ban last month
Ban seen as major impediment to Iran hosting 2019 Asian Cup
Iran was in running for 2019 Asian Cup
Tournament awarded to United Arab Emirates

stories.story: The ban on women attending matches came under the spotlight at the Asian Cup in Australia earlier this year, when thousands of female Iranian fans watched their soccer team without restriction. During the match against Iraq, activists called for the ban to end and unfurled a banner showing the face of Ghoncheh Ghavami. Ghavami, a British-Iranian woman, was detained in Iran last year while trying to watch a volleyball match. Iranian officials denied that Ghavami was arrested for attending the volleyball game, saying she was taken into custody for "anti-Iran activities." The news agency Reuters reported that she was recently pardoned by the Court of Appeal.
key_facts: Ban under spotlight at Asian Cup in Australia earlier this year
Thousands of female Iranian fans watched without restriction
Activists unfurled banner for Ghoncheh Ghavami during match against Iraq
Ghoncheh Ghavami, a British-Iranian woman, detained last year trying to watch volleyball
Iranian officials denied arrest for attending game, cited "anti-Iran activities"
Reuters reported Ghavami was recently pardoned by Court of Appeal
